audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Judges / Shoftim Song of Deborah war poem, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for archaic Hebrew poetry, YHWH theophany, Deborah's rise as mother in Israel, tribal participation and nonparticipation, Kishon battle imagery, Meroz curse, Jael blessing and killing, Sisera's mother scene, and forty-year quiet.

verses.restored: Deborah and Barak son of Abinoam sang on that day, saying:
When locks were loosed/leaders led in Israel, when people freely offered themselves, bless YHWH.
Hear, kings; give ear, rulers. I, to YHWH, I will sing; I will make melody to YHWH, Divine of Israel.
YHWH, when you went out from Seir, when you marched from field of Edom, earth quaked, heavens also dripped, clouds also dripped water.
Mountains flowed before YHWH, this Sinai, before YHWH, Divine of Israel.
In days of Shamgar son of Anath, in days of Jael, roads ceased, and walkers of paths went by crooked roads.
Villages/warriors ceased in Israel, they ceased, until I, Deborah, arose; I arose as mother in Israel.
They chose new gods; then war was in gates. Was shield or spear seen among forty thousand in Israel?
My heart is toward commanders of Israel, those freely offering themselves among people. Bless YHWH.
Riders on white/dappled donkeys, sitters on carpets, and walkers on way, speak.
From sound of those dividing at watering places, there they recount righteous acts of YHWH, righteous acts of his villages/warriors in Israel. Then people of YHWH went down to gates.
Awake, awake, Deborah! Awake, awake, speak a song! Rise, Barak, and take captive your captives, son of Abinoam!
Then remnant came down to nobles; people of YHWH came down for me against mighty ones.
From Ephraim their root was in Amalek; after you, Benjamin, among your peoples. From Machir commanders came down, and from Zebulun those bearing staff of scribe.
Princes in Issachar were with Deborah; Issachar was so with Barak, sent into valley at his feet. Among divisions of Reuben were great resolves of heart.
Why did you sit among sheepfolds, to hear piping for flocks? Among divisions of Reuben were great searchings of heart.
Gilead stayed across Jordan; Dan, why did he sojourn by ships? Asher sat by shore of seas and stayed by his landings.
Zebulun was a people that scorned its soul/life to death, and Naphtali, upon heights of field.
Kings came, they fought; then kings of Canaan fought at Taanach by waters of Megiddo. Gain of silver they did not take.
From heavens stars fought; from their courses they fought against Sisera.
Wadi Kishon swept them away, ancient wadi, wadi Kishon. March on, my soul/life, with strength.
Then horse hooves hammered from galloping, galloping of his mighty ones.
Curse Meroz, said messenger of YHWH; curse, curse its inhabitants, because they did not come to help YHWH, to help YHWH against mighty ones.
Most blessed of women is Jael, wife of Heber the Kenite; above women in tent she is blessed.
Water he asked; milk she gave. In a bowl of nobles she brought curds.
Her hand she sent to tent peg, and her right hand to workman's hammer. She hammered Sisera, crushed his head, shattered and pierced his temple.
Between her feet he bowed, fell, lay; between her feet he bowed, fell. Where he bowed, there he fell, destroyed.
Through window she looked out and cried, mother of Sisera through lattice: Why is his chariot delayed in coming? Why are hoofbeats of his chariots late?
Wise princesses answered her; indeed she returned answer to herself:
Are they not finding and dividing spoil? A womb, two wombs for every man; spoil of dyed garments for Sisera, spoil of dyed embroidered garments, dyed double-embroidered garments for necks of spoil.
So may all your enemies perish, YHWH; but those loving him be like sun going out in its might. And land was quiet forty years.

translation_notes: Judges 5 is among the oldest and most difficult Hebrew poems in the Bible; many lines contain rare vocabulary, compressed syntax, and debated tribal/geographic references.
The opening blessing may refer to loosened hair or leaders leading; both fit scenes of voluntary mobilization and require philological audit.
Deborah's self-description as 'mother in Israel' is public leadership imagery, not a reduction of women to biological motherhood.

The poem praises tribes that joined and critiques those that did not. It must not become a modern template for shaming civilians, conscientious objectors, disabled people, caregivers, or noncombatants for not entering war.
Stars fighting and Kishon sweeping away enemies are cosmic war-poetry images, not astronomical claims or weather prooftexts.
The curse of Meroz is war-poetry rhetoric and must not authorize cursing, violence, or communal punishment against people who refuse militarized participation.
Jael's blessing is poetic celebration of a killing. Her agency and the end of Sisera's violence are visible, but the act remains graphic and non-generalizable.

Sisera's mother's scene is bitter irony: she imagines rape and plunder as the reason for delay. The line exposes war's sexual violence without endorsing it; the phrase 'womb, two wombs' is deliberately dehumanizing and must not be softened into harmless spoil.
safety_note: Do not use Judges 5 to glorify war, shame noncombatants, curse communities, promote sexual violence, mock grieving mothers, or turn Jael's killing into a general model. Preserve Deborah's leadership, voluntary courage, and the poem's exposure of war brutality while keeping its violent triumphalism under moral restraint.
